## [00:00 - 03:02]

Hey guys, welcome to today's tutorial. So what we're going to look at is basically setting up an e-commerce campaign. This one is for a children's clothing brand. It's a high quality, very very good branding behind it children's brand. They sell in the UK and they sell in Denmark. They haven't really been successful with Facebook ads in the past. So what I'm going to do on this training video is actually show you how I would roll out a campaign for them and you'll see me set it up from start to finish. So the first thing that we need to do guys before we cut our teeth into any client campaign is basically mind map what that funnel is going to look like for that client, right? So this is what you're looking at at the moment. Now if I... Ah, it's not going to work. So if I... This was built out of Lucidchart. It's a really really great software, highly recommended. And basically what we do in here is we'll mind map exactly the funnel structure, the ad sets that we need in there, the targeting, the placements, the spend so that any one time our team can come and refer to this and the client is also across what is happening with their campaigns, how much they're spending every day. And what the key metrics are for the campaigns that we're running as well. So I'm going to go through this funnel first and explain it to you. And then what I'm going to do is take you into ads manager and we're going to start building this funnel. And this funnel is actually going to need to have some bits and bobs added to it as we go along. So essentially for e-commerce what is working really really well for these guys is a piece of content at the top of the funnel. Now typically this is a video, then you'll have a middle of a funnel sort of consideration phase if you will where you're driving really high quality clicks to their website. And then you've typically got DPA at the bottom of the funnel. So dynamic product ads at the bottom of the funnel. And this is retargeting people based on the stuff that they viewed on the website. It's retargeting people that abandon their shopping cart. And the purpose of that last bit is to obviously get people to buy. So let's go through this together. So over here you'll see we've got the three parts of the funnel and then we've got a stand alone here which I'll explain to you in a second. So top of funnel, the campaign is simply the campaign objective. So you'll see here we've got a conversion campaign and then over here we have a DPA. And this one is PPE. That is really an acronym that we use in the industry for page post engagement. So it's an engagement campaign. And the objective of this campaign is that we want to go out with a really good piece of content and we want to segment our audience.

## [03:02 - 06:05]

So we want our audience to identify themselves as being interested in what we have to offer by them showing engagement with the stuff that we are rolling out, the content that we have. So this is a cold audience, right? These are going to be people that have never interacted with or engaged with the brand before. And this is the top of our bucket. We want lots and lots of people coming in, cold audience. And for this particular client we're rolling with a look alike. Lookalikes are really, really good. But because her lookalike only had the seed audience, meaning the starting audience only had, I think, 500 people in it or 400 people in it, we've layered it on with interest. We've also created exclusions in there and post codes. So we've added wealthy neighborhood post codes because her products are not that cheap. And that is because lookalikes with seed audiences of around a thousand or less don't tend to work as well as those with a thousand plus, right? A thousand plus seed audience lookalikes, meaning the beginning audience that you created that lookalike from had over a thousand people, are very, very high quality in general. But hers was very, very small, so we've layered it on with some different segments in there. Now we're going to exclude people that purchased and we're also going to exclude people that visited the website because we don't want these people to end up right back at the beginning of the funnel, right? If someone's visited the website, they should be in this part. They should be getting retargeted with our DPA ads. For this one, we're going to do a placement of mobile. And the reason for that is that mobile has probably a lot more real estate than most other placements except for Instagram and audience network. So it is hot quality, typically, generally speaking. And it's got a lot of real estate meaning, I guess what I'm trying to get at is that you can get really, really cheap results through mobile. Now guys, this is obviously a generalization. Nothing in Facebook is ever steadfast, so make sure you look at the previous data of your client to see which placement would be a better option for them. In our situation, mobile, she didn't have much data to begin with, so mobile is a great place to start. And what we're going to do is spend $10 a day here. Now this is very, very small budgets, unfortunately, but that's okay. We're going to make it work. So we're going to spend $10 a day in here. And I don't know why these didn't color code, but we've got two ads. We're going to roll a video ad and we're going to roll a PPE ad, just a normal page post ad. And I'm going to take you through and create those in front of you as well. Now for those of you that are wanting to split test a lot of creative, because you may be thinking, well, why are you only testing out two different pieces of creative?

## [06:05 - 09:05]

You're sort of putting all your eggs in one basket here. Facebook has this really quirky, funny little thing where when you first roll out a campaign, it will automatically optimize for an ad creative or two within the first sort of two to three hours. So it starts to feed all the budget into one ad. It just picks favorites. But a lot of the times there's no rhyme or reason. It's not necessarily the best performing ad. It's not necessarily the one that engages the most people. So typically if you're going to roll out six different creatives, four of them are not even going to get fed any of the budget, because Facebook has basically picked a favorite within the first two to three hours. So if you do need to test out six creatives, what I recommend is that you just have two of them live at once and then you rotate through them. So pause the first two after they have reached 5,000 people and then turn two other ones on, test them in this manner, et cetera, et cetera, and you roll through them in this way. So if you go live with six ads at once, Facebook is not really going to feed that equally. So our key metrics, and this is internally for us as a team, what we want to look for is we want to make sure that the video is engaging our audience so that there actually is an audience that is sticking around and watching 10 seconds of that video. So we're creating a custom audience of people that viewed 10 seconds of that video. But we need to make sure that that is actually happening. So some videos, when you go into the insights for your videos, and I'll show you how we do that in a minute, you'll see a very, very big drop off within the first 10 seconds. And if that's the case, you might not even be getting an audience, in which case the targeting needs to be reviewed for that video or the creative is not quite right for that audience. And then for our PPE campaign, we're aiming for a 30 cent page post engagement. We would also really love to drive link clicks, so that's something we're going to watch out for here as well. In the middle of the funnel, we have a conversion campaign. Now, typically I would recommend that you run a link click campaign here, because this is going to be a very small audience. We're only spending $10 a day here. There's going to be a thousand people max at any one time in this audience. And usually when you're dealing with small audiences, you do not need to run a conversion campaign. You don't need to do that because you don't need Facebook to optimize for that. It's such a small audience that you're going to reach them anyways, right? So you don't need the OCPM part of the algorithm that optimizes for a conversion, because it's such a small audience. That's normally what you do for like 500,000, 800,000 sized audiences.

## [09:05 - 12:07]

Typically what we say is audiences 50,000 and under, you're better off either running a link click campaign or optimizing for link clicks, because you don't need the OCPM part of the algorithm. And our objective here is that we want to drive really high quality clicks to the site, right? You need to make sure the website's converting at 2%, not 20%, 2% website conversion rate is the standard for e-commerce.
